html:
RECOUP Integrative Root-Cause Rehabilitation Program for Parkinson's Disease

Parkinson's Disease (PD) is the second most common neurodegenerative disorder worldwide, affecting over 10 million individuals globally (Parkinson's Foundation, 2023). It is characterized by the progressive loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, resulting in a dopamine deficit in the nigrostriatal pathway, a key modulator of motor control. In India, PD prevalence is estimated to range between 70 to 328 per 100,000 population, with a significant rise among individuals over the age of 60 (Kumar et al., 2022).

It results in cardinal motor features (bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, postural instability) and a spectrum of non-motor symptoms (anosmia, constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder, depression, cognitive impairment, and autonomic dysfunction).

While PD was traditionally understood as a dopaminergic motor disorder, emerging research demonstrates that it is a multi-system, multi-etiological disorder involving:

  • Alpha-synuclein aggregation (Lewy pathology) spreads from the gut and olfactory bulb to the brainstem and cortex (Braak's Hypothesis).
  • Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress contribute to neuronal apoptosis (Schapira et al., 2011).
  • Gut dysbiosis and increased intestinal permeability, which initiate systemic inflammation and may precede motor symptoms by over a decade (Sampson et al., 2016; Bedarf et al., 2017).
  • Environmental neurotoxins, such as pesticides, heavy metals, moulds, and solvents, are associated with an increased risk of PD (Tanner et al., 2011; Goldman et al., 2012).
  • Impaired autophagy and protein degradation systems contribute to intracellular accumulation of misfolded alpha-synuclein (Menzies et al., 2017).

Conventional treatment primarily relies on dopaminergic replacement therapy, particularly levodopa-carbidopa, MAO-B inhibitors, and dopamine agonists. While these pharmacological agents provide symptomatic relief, they do not modify disease progression, and long-term use is associated with motor complications or extrapyramidal symptoms such as dyskinesias and motor fluctuations (Olanow et al., 2004; Fox et al., 2018).

Evidence now supports that integrative interventions, including targeted nutrition, mitochondrial support, gut-brain axis restoration, neurorehabilitation, and regenerative medicine, can improve outcomes and quality of life in PD patients (Titova & Chaudhuri, 2018; Mischley et al., 2017).

At Recoup Health, we combine these emerging insights with over two decades of experience in neurorehabilitation, functional medicine, and integrative care to create a 4R-based integrative root-cause rehabilitation program that addresses not only symptoms but also upstream biological imbalances, functional impairments, and regenerative potential.

Recoup's Integrative, Root-Cause Rehabilitation approach addresses these elements systematically using our 4R protocol: Relief of symptoms, Root Cause Redressal, Restoration, and Regeneration.

The Recoup 4R Protocol represents a clinically comprehensive and holistic model designed to enhance neuroprotection, functional independence, and long-term resilience in Parkinson's disease.

R1: Relief of Symptoms

The acute phase of PD is marked by motor impairments (bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity, postural instability) and non-motor symptoms (depression, fatigue, sleep dysfunction, constipation, cognitive decline). This phase aims to achieve symptom relief, independence in activities of daily living (ADLs), and improved functional mobility. Suggested interventions in this phase include:

  1. Neuro-education: Education on PD progression, neuroplasticity, and patient empowerment through structured routines and self-care strategies.
  2. Pharmacotherapy: Conventional Anti-Parkinsonism Drugs like Levodopa + Carbidopa, Dopamine Agonists, MAO-B Inhibitors, COMT Inhibitors, Anticholinergics, Amantadine, Cholinesterase inhibitors, along with appropriate supplements.
  3. Physical Therapy: LSVT-BIG (Lee Silverman Voice Treatment) Protocol for motor activation, Proprioceptive training, and Frenkel Exercises for gait freezing, Balance retraining, fall prevention programs, postural control, and gait re-education. Stretching and mobility drills to reduce rigidity.
  4. Aquatic Therapy: Resistance-based movement training, postural control, and relaxation.
  5. Body Weight Supported Treadmill Training: For improving gait rhythm, step length, and endurance.
  6. Whole Body Vibration Therapy: For postural instability, muscle activation, and fall reduction.
  7. Hippotherapy (horse simulator): Trunk control and balance improvement.
  8. Tai Chi, Qi Gong, and Yoga: Improve proprioception, stability, mindfulness, and respiratory control.
  9. Franklin Method: Neurocognitive imagery for correcting motor patterns and improving body awareness.
  10. Occupational Therapy (OT): ADL re-training, Fine motor control, adaptive tools training, Energy conservation, and home safety adaptations.
  11. Speech and Swallowing Therapy: LSVT-LOUD for hypophonia, Expiratory Muscle Strength Training (EMST), and dysphagia management
  12. Exergaming and Smart Devices: Smart Playball, VR tools for cognitive-motor integration (e.g., attention, visuospatial skills, hand-eye coordination).
  13. Sleep Optimization: Sleep coaching, supplementation, CBTi, circadian retraining.
  14. Colon Hydrotherapy: For bowel regulation and relief from constipation (common in PD).
  15. Anti-inflammatory Nutrition
  16. Psychological First Aid: CBT-based interventions, Pain Reprocessing Therapy, emotional regulation strategies, and caregiver psychoeducation.

R2: Root Cause Redressal

Focuses on investigating and addressing upstream biological, microbial, environmental, and metabolic contributors to the pathophysiology of Parkinson's disease. Suggested interventions include:

  1. Comprehensive Functional Testing: Gut microbiome and intestinal permeability (zonulin, calprotectin), Heavy metals, pesticides, and mycotoxins, Nutrient panel (CoQ10, Vitamin D, B vitamins, magnesium, Omega-3), Mitochondrial function (organic acids, lactate/pyruvate ratio), Neurotransmitter and hormone profiling.
  2. Colon Hydrotherapy: Gut detoxification, rebalancing microbiota, and bowel clearance.
  3. Red Light Therapy (Far and Near Infrared): For mitochondrial activation, ATP production, and microglial modulation.
  4. Cold and Heat Exposure: Infrared sauna, cold showers, and contrast therapy to induce autophagy, improve detoxification, and stress resilience.
  5. Breathwork and Vagal Nerve Stimulation: HRV biofeedback, resonance breathing, and cold face immersion for parasympathetic activation.
  6. PEMF Helmet Therapy (Pulsed Electromagnetic Field Therapy): Non-invasive brain stimulation using low-frequency, low-intensity electromagnetic fields delivered through a helmet to target motor and prefrontal cortical areas.
  7. Exercise Therapy with HRV Biofeedback: Heart-rate regulated aerobic sessions with HRV optimization to reduce neuroinflammation.
  8. Anti-Inflammatory and Neuroprotective Nutrition:
  9. Mitochondrial Enhancement Therapy: IV Nutrition (CoQ10, NAD+, B vitamins, magnesium, ALA), Oral glutathione, acetyl-L-carnitine
  10. Mind-Body Therapies: EMDR for trauma processing, DBT and EFT for emotional regulation and nervous system resetting.
  11. Ayurveda Detoxification: Panchakarma therapies (basti, virechana) to remove toxins linked to neurodegeneration.

R3: Restoring Physiological Balances

Focuses on the reintegration of disrupted systemic networks, supporting metabolic health, neuromuscular function, and emotional resilience. Suggested Interventions during this phase:

  1. Advanced Neurorehabilitation: Coordination Dynamics Therapy (CDT), Virtual and Augmented Reality-based movement retraining, Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) for tremor and rigidity, Neurofeedback for emotional and cognitive optimization, HRV-based breath retraining
  2. Alexander Technique and Feldenkrais Method: To re-educate movement patterns, enhance proprioception, and increase fluidity of movement.
  3. Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM): Detection and correction of dysglycemia, insulin resistance management.
  4. IV Micronutrient Therapy: Glutathione, Vitamin C, B-complex, taurine, magnesium, and amino acid blends
  5. Treatment of Chronic Infections and Toxins: If identified, use protocols including chelation, binders, and antimicrobials
  6. Occupational Therapy and Ergonomic Support/Home Modifications
  7. Hormone Profile Review and Replacement (if needed):
  8. Psychological Empowerment: Purpose therapy, values-based journaling, Group resilience workshops, and structured group therapies
  9. Speech and Language Therapy (Advanced): Cognitive-Communicative Training, Fluency and Prosody Retraining, Script Training and Conversational Coaching, Voice-Assisted Technology, Swallowing Rehabilitation.
  10. Mirror Therapy: Visual feedback using the unaffected limb to promote motor imagery and cortical reorganization.

R4: Regeneration and Longevity

Focuses on supporting long-term neuroprotection, cellular renewal, slowing progression, and building resilience. Suggested Interventions during this phase:

  1. Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT): Enhances neurogenesis, stem cell activation, and oxygen delivery to hypometabolic brain regions
  2. Ozone Therapy and Molecular Hydrogen: Used to modulate oxidative stress and immunoinflammatory status
  3. IV Nutrition for Regeneration: NAD+, Glutathione, and regenerative nutrient blends
  4. Peptide Therapies
  5. Autophagy Enhancement Protocols:Intermittent fasting (16:8 or 18:6), Polyphenol-rich botanicals: spermidine, quercetin, resveratrol
  6. Anti-Aging Interventions: Telomere-preserving nutrients
  7. Frailty Index Monitoring: VO2 max, HRV tracking, grip strength, gait speed, cognition tracking (MoCA)
  8. Relapse and Decline Prevention Planning: Personalized action plans for worsening episodes, Regular telehealth monitoring via Recoup EPHR, Crisis preparedness, Health coaching, and caregiver support

Recoup Health's Credentials

  • Dr. Deepak Sharan has over 25 years of experience treating Parkinson's disease (PD).
  • He is a Consultant in Orthopaedic Surgery, Rehabilitation, Orthopaedic Rheumatology, Pain, Musculoskeletal, Regenerative, Functional and Lifestyle Medicine, Ergonomics, and Occupational Health.
  • Diplomate of the International Board of Lifestyle Medicine (Dip IBLM).
  • Certified Functional Medicine Physician from the Institute for Functional Medicine, USA, and the Functional Medicine Academy, USA. Trained in the Wahl's Protocol by Dr Terry Wahls.
  • Certificate in Anti-Ageing and Precision Medicine (The American Academy of Anti-Aging Medicine).
  • Advanced courses in Functional Medicine, Kharrazian Institute, USA - Neuroinflammation, Neurodegeneration, Gastrointestinal, Autoimmunity, Cognitive Decline and Dementia, Chronic Fatigue and Pain Syndromes, Mood and Anxiety Disorders, Longevity and Wellness Optimisation.
  • Other RECOUP team members are trained by him.
